questions.q: What is the difference between an image and a container?
questions.a: An image is a read-only template; a container is a running instance of that image.

questions.q: What Linux feature isolates process IDs inside a container?
What Linux feature limits CPU and memory usage for a container?

questions.a: A host path mounted into the container filesystem.

questions.q: What does PID 1 do in a container?
questions.a: It acts as the init process and must reap orphaned children.

questions.q: What does multi-stage build let you do?
questions.a: Use one build stage for compilation and a smaller final image for runtime.

questions.q: Why does PID 1 need special handling in a container?
questions.a: It must forward signals and reap zombies or processes can linger.
They let Docker reuse unchanged steps and speed up rebuilds.

questions.q: Why are distroless or slim images safer than full OS images?
questions.a: They reduce attack surface and remove unused tooling.

questions.q: Why does rootless container mode help security?
questions.a: The daemon and containers run without full root privileges on the host.

questions.q: Why can overlay filesystems make containers feel fast to start?
questions.a: Most layers are shared and only the writable layer is unique.

questions.q: Why is `docker exec` not the same as starting a new container?
questions.a: It joins an existing container namespace and does not create new isolation.

questions.q: When should you choose containers over virtual machines?
questions.a: When you want lighter isolation, fast startup, and dense packaging of services.

questions.q: When is a bind mount the wrong choice?
questions.a: When you need portability or do not want the container to depend on host paths.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with small base images?
questions.a: You get a smaller attack surface, but debugging and tooling become harder.

questions.q: When would you use an init process inside a container?
questions.a: When your app spawns children and needs correct signal forwarding and reaping.

questions.q: Why might you avoid containers for a stateful, low-change legacy app?
questions.a: The operational complexity may outweigh the packaging benefits.

questions.q: What is the tradeoff of setting hard resource limits?
questions.a: They improve isolation but can cause throttling or OOM kills under load.
